Define the interface with appropriate methods including any parameters that implementations need for their logic. Activate the definition. This is typically done when creating a custom extensible framework — standard SAP BAdI definitions are already shipped with filter configurations by SAP.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Custom framework requirement: multiple teams needed to plug their validation logic into a central invoice processing Z-program without touching each other's code. Designed a custom filter-dependent BAdI — each team creates their own implementation filtered by BUKRS. Central program calls one GET BADI — all relevant implementations fire. Clean architecture.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Forgetting to enable Multiple Use when expecting multiple implementations — without it, only one implementation can be active at a time, defeating the purpose of filter-dependent BAdI.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE18: Create filter-dependent BAdI (for custom framework)
" BAdI Name: ZBADI_INVOICE_PROCESS
" Properties tab:
"   Filter-Dependent: [X]
"   Filter Type:      BUKRS
"   Multiple Use:     [X]  ← CRITICAL for multiple implementations
" Interface tab:
"   Interface Name:   IF_EX_ZBADI_INVOICE_PROCESS  (auto-generated)
" Methods tab:
"   Method: PROCESS_INVOICE
"     Param: IV_BUKRS (Type BUKRS, Importing)
"     Param: IV_AMOUNT (Type WRBTR, Importing)
"     Param: CV_RESULT (Type STRING, Changing)

followupAnswerBank.question: Can one BAdI implementation handle multiple filter values?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Haan — SE19 mein ek implementation ke liye multiple filter values assign kar sakte ho. Jaise: ZFI_TAX_BENELUX implementation ke liye filter values: BUKRS = '3000' (Belgium), BUKRS = '4000' (Netherlands), BUKRS = '5000' (Luxembourg). Kyunki teeno countries ki VAT rules similar hain, ek implementation teen filter values handle kar sakti hai. SE19 mein implementation create karo → Filter tab → multiple rows add karo different filter values ke liye. Yeh duplication avoid karta hai similar logic ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Yes — in SE19, a single BAdI implementation can be configured for multiple filter values. In the Filter tab of the implementation, add multiple rows each with a different filter value. For example, an implementation for Benelux countries can have filter values for company codes of Belgium, Netherlands, and Luxembourg — all three trigger the same implementation class. This is efficient when multiple filter values share identical or near-identical logic. Each filter value row is a separate entry but all point to the same implementation class.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Multinational rollout: 12 countries, but 4 distinct tax logic groups. Created 4 implementations — each covering 2-4 company codes with similar tax rules. Reduced 12 potential implementations to 4. Maintenance much simpler.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Creating a separate implementation for each company code when logic is identical — unnecessary duplication. One implementation can cover multiple filter values. Review commonalities before creating new implementations.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE19: One implementation → multiple filter values
" Implementation: ZFI_TAX_BENELUX
" Filter tab entries:
"   Row 1: BUKRS = '3000'  (Belgium)
"   Row 2: BUKRS = '4000'  (Netherlands)
"   Row 3: BUKRS = '5000'  (Luxembourg)
" All three company codes → same class: ZCL_FI_TAX_BENELUX
"
" Calling code: GET BADI lo_badi FILTERS bukrs = '4000'
" → Loads ZCL_FI_TAX_BENELUX (matches row 2)
" Same class handles all three Benelux company codes

followupAnswerBank.question: What happens when no implementation matches the runtime filter value?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Jab GET BADI FILTERS bukrs = '9999' call hoti hai aur koi implementation filter 9999 match nahi karti — system CX_BADI_NOT_IMPLEMENTED exception raise karta hai. Agar calling code mein TRY-CATCH nahi hai toh runtime dump. Agar TRY-CATCH hai toh CATCH block execute hoga — wahan typically default processing hoti hai ya graceful skip. Isliye hamesha TRY-CATCH mandatory hai filter-dependent BAdI ke saath — especially kyunki new company codes future mein add ho sakte hain jinka implementation nahi bana hoga yet.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When GET BADI with a filter value finds no matching active implementation, it raises the exception CX_BADI_NOT_IMPLEMENTED. Without a TRY-CATCH block around GET BADI and CALL BADI, this causes a runtime dump. The catch block should handle this gracefully — either applying a default processing path, logging a warning, or silently continuing. This is especially important in filter-dependent BAdIs because new organizational units like company codes or plants may be added in future without corresponding implementations yet configured. The TRY-CATCH is not optional — it is a mandatory pattern for production-safe BAdI usage.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: New company code added to SAP system for a subsidiary. No one remembered to create BAdI implementation for it. First transaction posted — dump. CX_BADI_NOT_IMPLEMENTED. CATCH block was present — defaulted to standard tax rate. Business continued. Implementation created next sprint. Proper fallback saved the day.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Omitting TRY-CATCH on filter-dependent BAdI calls. In global systems with many company codes, it is almost certain that some company code will eventually have no matching implementation. Defensive coding is mandatory.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" MANDATORY pattern for filter-dependent BAdI calls:
DATA: lo_validator TYPE REF TO if_ex_zbadi_tax_validation.
TRY.
    GET BADI lo_validator
      FILTERS bukrs = lv_bukrs.        " May raise exception
    CALL BADI lo_validator->validate_tax
      EXPORTING iv_amount = lv_amount
      IMPORTING cv_tax   = lv_tax_amt.
  CATCH cx_badi_not_implemented.
    " No implementation for this company code
    " Apply standard default rate
    lv_tax_amt = lv_amount * '0.10'.   " Default 10%
    MESSAGE |No tax BAdI for &{ lv_bukrs }| TYPE 'W'.
ENDTRY.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does filter-dependent BAdI differ from a regular multi-use BAdI?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Regular multi-use BAdI: Multiple implementations active ho sakti hain, sab ek saath execute hoti hain, koi filter nahi — sab implementations chhengi har call pe. Filter-dependent BAdI: Multiple implementations active ho sakti hain, lekin runtime filter value se decide hota hai ki kaunsi implementation(s) execute hongi — sirf matching wali chalti hai. Ek practical difference: regular multi-use mein agar 5 implementations hain, 5 execute hongi. Filter-dependent mein agar 5 implementations hain aur sirf 1 filter match karti hai, sirf 1 execute hogi. Resource efficiency aur separation of concerns ke liye filter-dependent better hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A regular multiple-use BAdI executes all active implementations for every call regardless of context — all five active implementations run every time. A filter-dependent BAdI evaluates the runtime filter value against each implementation's filter configuration and executes only the matching implementation or implementations. This selective execution provides both performance efficiency — unnecessary implementations are not loaded — and logical separation — each implementation handles only its relevant context. Filter-dependent BAdIs are architecturally cleaner for multi-company or multi-country scenarios because they enforce context-specific execution without requiring conditional logic inside implementations.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Before filter-dependent BAdI was available/used: one implementation with 20 IF-ELSEIF blocks for 20 company codes. Maintenance nightmare — every new country required touching the same class. After refactoring to filter-dependent: 20 separate implementations, each touching only their country logic. New country = new implementation only, existing ones untouched.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using regular multi-use BAdI for multi-company logic and adding IF company_code = X inside implementations — this is exactly the problem filter-dependent BAdIs solve. Recognize the pattern and use the right tool.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Regular multi-use BAdI: ALL implementations run
" 5 active implementations → all 5 execute every call
GET BADI lo_badi.  " No filter
CALL BADI lo_badi->process.  " All 5 fire

" Filter-dependent BAdI: ONLY matching impl runs
" 5 active implementations, runtime bukrs = '1000'
GET BADI lo_badi FILTERS bukrs = lv_bukrs.  " Filter: '1000'
" Only implementation with bukrs = '1000' executes
" Implementations for '2000','3000','4000','5000' skipped
CALL BADI lo_badi->validate.  " Only 1 of 5 fires

followupAnswerBank.question: What filter types can be used in filter-dependent BAdIs?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Filter type koi bhi DDIC domain ya elementary type ho sakta hai. Common ones jo SAP ne standard BAdIs mein use kiye hain: BUKRS — company code, VKORG — sales organization, WERKS — plant, LAND1 — country, SPRAS — language key, MANDT — client (usually not used as filter directly). Custom filter types bhi possible hain — agar tum apna Z-BAdI banate ho toh apna custom domain bhi filter type ban sakta hai. Filter type determines what values can be used for filtering — it provides the value range for the filter.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Filter types in BAdI definitions can be any DDIC domain or elementary data type. Standard SAP uses common organizational unit types: BUKRS for company code filtering, VKORG for sales organization, WERKS for plant, LAND1 for country, and SPRAS for language-based filtering. Custom Z-BAdI definitions can use any custom domain as filter type, enabling highly specific filtering like customer classification, product category, or business area. The filter type determines the domain of valid values and how the runtime filter value is compared against implementation filter configurations. Choosing the right filter type is important for both performance and logical accuracy of the filtering.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Custom BAdI for pricing: used VKORG (sales org) as filter. Different sales organizations had different pricing strategies. 8 sales orgs, 4 distinct pricing logic groups — 4 implementations, filter value ranges assigned. Much cleaner than a pricing-logic-routing table.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using MANDT as filter type — client-dependent BAdIs don't need client filter as SAP already handles client separation. Also avoid overly granular filter types that create too many implementations — group similar logic where possible.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you configure filter values in SE19 for a filter-dependent BAdI?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SE19 mein implementation create karo. Sabse important tab hai Filter tab — yahan filter values configure karte hain. Tab mein table grid hoti hai: filter field name aur value. Ek ya multiple rows add karo — each row ek filter value. Jaise: BUKRS = 1000 (ek row). Ya BUKRS = 1000 aur BUKRS = 2000 (do rows — dono filter karo same implementation se). Range bhi possible hai kuch filter types ke liye: FROM 1000 TO 1999 — 1000-1999 range sab ek implementation se handle. Save aur activate karo implementation. Filter configuration transport ke saath move hoti hai — ENHO object mein included hoti hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: In SE19, after selecting the BAdI and creating the implementation class, navigate to the Filter tab of the implementation. Add filter value rows — each row specifies a value for the configured filter type. For example, add BUKRS equals 1000 for India and a second row BUKRS equals 2000 for Germany if both have identical logic. Some filter types support ranges — from value to value — allowing a single implementation to cover a contiguous range of organizational units. Save and activate the implementation. The filter configuration is part of the ENHO object and gets transported with the implementation, ensuring consistent filter behavior across landscapes.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Initial setup: created implementation but forgot to add filter value in Filter tab. Implementation was active but GET BADI with any filter value never matched it — zero execution. SE19 Filter tab was empty. Added BUKRS = '1000' — immediate fix. Classic gotcha for first-timers.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Activating implementation but leaving Filter tab empty — implementation will never match any runtime filter value and will never execute. Always verify Filter tab has at least one value entry before testing.

followupAnswerBank.question: How are filter-dependent BAdIs used in S/4HANA global rollouts?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: S/4HANA global rollout mein filter-dependent BAdI architecture kaafi common design pattern hai. Scenario: 20 countries, sabki alag legal compliance, tax rules, posting logic. Option A (bad): Ek BAdI, ek implementation class, 20 IF-ELSEIF country blocks — spaghetti code, one change affects all. Option B (good): Ek BAdI definition, 20 filter-configured implementations (ya 7-8 regional ones with multi-value filter), clean separation. Maintenance: new country add karo — sirf new implementation banao, existing ones untouched. Regression risk: zero for existing countries jab new country adds hoti hai. Upgrade: BAdI definition survives, implementations survive. Yeh architecture scalable global SAP deployments ke liye preferred pattern hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: In S/4HANA global rollouts, filter-dependent BAdIs are the architectural foundation for country-specific or region-specific customization. A single BAdI definition handles the extension point while each country or regional implementation contains country-specific logic activated only for that country's company code or sales organization. New country onboarding means creating a new implementation — no existing implementations are touched, eliminating regression risk. This pattern supports parallel development where country teams can develop and transport their implementations independently. The pattern scales from 2 countries to 50 without architectural changes. Combined with transport management, it enables a clean template approach where core logic stays generic and country-specific logic is isolated in filter-specific implementations.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: 15-country S/4HANA rollout: one pricing BAdI, 15 filter-configured implementations. Each country team owned their implementation. Core pricing template untouched across 2 years of rollout. No cross-country regression issues. Template approach validated by project success.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Designing global systems with if-else country logic in single implementation — creates dependencies between country teams and makes parallel development impossible. Filter-dependent BAdI removes this bottleneck.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Global rollout architecture:
" One BAdI: ZBADI_COUNTRY_TAX_CALC
" Filter: BUKRS (company code)
"
" Implementations (parallel development by country teams):
" ZFI_TAX_IN  → BUKRS = '1000' (India   — GST 18%)
" ZFI_TAX_DE  → BUKRS = '2000' (Germany — VAT 19%)
" ZFI_TAX_US  → BUKRS = '3000' (USA     — State tax)
" ZFI_TAX_AU  → BUKRS = '4000' (AUS     — GST 10%)
" ZFI_TAX_BNL → BUKRS IN (5000,6000,7000) (Benelux)
"
" Core calling code — UNCHANGED for all countries:
GET BADI lo_tax FILTERS bukrs = lv_bukrs.
CALL BADI lo_tax->calc_tax EXPORTING iv_amt = lv_amt.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the performance impact of filter-dependent BAdIs versus regular multi-use BAdIs?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Filter-dependent BAdI mein system sirf matching implementation(s) load aur execute karta hai — non-matching implementations completely bypassed hoti hain, unka class loading overhead bhi nahi. Regular multi-use BAdI mein sab active implementations load aur execute hoti hain — 10 implementations hain toh 10 class loads aur 10 method calls. Performance difference: zyada implementations hongi, filter-dependent approach proportionally zyada efficient hogi. Real-world scale par: 20-country global implementation mein filter-dependent approach 19 unnecessary class loads avoid karta hai per transaction — yeh meaningful overhead reduction hai high-volume transaction processing mein.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Filter-dependent BAdIs have a performance advantage over regular multi-use BAdIs in multi-implementation scenarios. The runtime loads only implementations whose filter value matches, bypassing all non-matching implementations entirely — no class instantiation, no method call overhead for non-matching implementations. In a system with 20 country-specific implementations, only one fires per transaction — nineteen are filtered out with minimal overhead. Regular multi-use BAdIs load and execute all active implementations regardless of relevance. For high-volume transactions like payment runs or mass posting, this filtering efficiency compounds across millions of calls. This is another reason filter-dependent BAdIs are preferred for global implementations beyond just code organization.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Performance comparison done on a batch job: regular multi-use BAdI with 12 implementations took 4.2 seconds per 1000 records. After refactoring to filter-dependent: 1.8 seconds per 1000 records — 57% improvement. The savings came purely from skipping 11 irrelevant class loads per record.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming BAdI call performance is negligible — at batch scale with millions of records, even small per-record savings multiply significantly. Always profile BAdI performance in batch scenarios before go-live.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Performance comparison at scale:
"
" Regular multi-use (all 20 implementations execute):
" 1 million records × 20 class loads × ~0.1ms each = ~2000 seconds
"
" Filter-dependent (only 1 implementation executes per record):
" 1 million records × 1 class load × ~0.1ms each = ~100 seconds
"
